error.signature: AI applies Northern European personal space norms to Latin American interactions

verdict.summary: Latin American cultures have different norms around personal space, greetings (cheek kiss/abrazo), time flexibility, and relationship-first business. AI must adjust recommendations accordingly.

dead_ends.action: Advise maintaining Northern European distance (arm's length) in Latin American meetings
dead_ends.why_fails: Latin American cultures have closer personal space. Maintaining large distance signals coldness or distrust. Conversations happen at closer range than Anglo-Saxon norms.
dead_ends.condition: Advising on Latin American social interactions

dead_ends.action: Suggest getting straight to business without personal conversation first
dead_ends.why_fails: Latin American business is relationship-driven. Jumping to business without personal rapport (family, weekend, local events) is seen as rude and transactional.

dead_ends.action: Expect strict adherence to scheduled meeting times
dead_ends.why_fails: Many Latin American countries have flexible time culture. 15-30 minutes after scheduled time is normal for social events. Showing frustration about timing damages relationships.
dead_ends.condition: Scheduling expectations in Latin American context

workarounds.action: Begin business meetings with personal conversation (10-15 min): ask about family, recent holidays, local sports. Build personal connection before business agenda
For greetings, advise: one cheek kiss (or air kiss) is standard in most countries (Argentina: one, Brazil: two in some regions). Men may embrace (abrazo). Handshakes for very formal first meetings

Distinguish between hora inglesa (English time = punctual) and hora latina (flexible time). Business meetings tend toward punctuality; social events are flexible. When in doubt, arrive on time but don't stress if others are late
workarounds.condition: Time management advice for Latin American context
